fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Core holding. This aggressive hybrid fund holds ~65% stocks + ~35% debt, giving you growth with a built-in safety net. Crucially, it qualifies as equity-taxed (LTCG 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25L), so at your 30% slab, this saves massive tax compared to pure debt funds. Think of it as a two-in-one fund — stocks for growth, debt for cushion.

fund.name: HSBC Equity Savings Fund - Direct Growth

allocations.rationale: Equity savings funds hold ~30-35% stocks + arbitrage + debt, keeping total equity above 65% (so equity-taxed). Lower volatility than a full equity fund — ideal for a conservative investor near retirement. Gives you market participation with a much smoother ride.

fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Large cap stocks are the most stable part of the stock market. This fund invests in India's top 100 companies (Reliance, HDFC, Infosys etc.) — blue-chip names that weather downturns better than smaller companies. This portion is earmarked for the inheritance goal — it has 15+ years to grow.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Growth - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Aditya Birla Sun Life AMC Limited

allocations.rationale: Pure debt for stability — this fund invests in government bonds and high-quality corporate papers. Returns are predictable and don't swing with the stock market. Note: gains are taxed at your 30% slab (no LTCG benefit), so we keep this allocation small. Think of it as your 'anchor' in storms.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Gold ETF
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Gold is your insurance against inflation and market crashes — it tends to rise when stocks fall. 10% allocation gives diversification without over-concentration. Note: requires a demat account. Gains are slab-taxed at 30%, so we keep this at a moderate level. Think of it as a financial shock absorber.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Your healthcare contingency bucket. Overnight funds invest in ultra-short-term loans (1 day) — near-zero risk of loss, and you can redeem within a day. The 5.5% return is low, but safety and instant access are what matter here. ₹20L earmarked as your ₹50L contingency (along with ₹30L from the Equity & Debt fund that's accessible after 1 year).

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open accounts — If you don't already have one, open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or MFU. You'll also need a demat account (Zerodha/Coin) for the Gold ETF. Complete KYC if not done.

Step 2: Deploy the ₹2Cr corpus — DON'T invest all at once. Spread over 6 months via STP (Systematic Transfer Plan): park the full amount in the ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund, then set up weekly transfers of ~₹8L into the other 5 funds. This averages your entry price and reduces the risk of investing everything at a market peak.

Step 3: Start monthly SIPs of ₹1L — Set up: ₹35K → ICICI Equity & Debt, ₹20K → HSBC Equity Savings, ₹15K → Nippon Large Cap, ₹10K → ABLSL Medium Term, ₹10K → ICICI Gold ETF, ₹10K → ICICI Overnight.

Step 4: Mark the healthcare bucket — Mentally earmark ₹20L in the Overnight Fund + ₹30L in the Equity & Debt Fund as your ₹50L healthcare contingency. Don't touch these unless needed.

Step 5: Choose Growth option for all funds — Dividends get reinvested automatically, which maximises compounding. Switch to IDCW (income distribution) option only after retirement if you need regular payouts.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if your STP has completed deploying the ₹2Cr. Verify all 6 SIPs are running on schedule. Don't check returns yet — it's too early.

After 1 year: Review each fund's performance vs. its category average. If any fund has consistently underperformed its peers by more than 3% per year, consider switching. Check if your healthcare bucket is intact.

At retirement (5 years): This is the big review. Assess total corpus. If it's above ₹3Cr, you're on track for inheritance goals. Start shifting 5% from Large Cap to Debt/Overnight to de-risk. Set up a Systematic Withdrawal Plan (SWP) from the Equity & Debt and Equity Savings funds for monthly income — withdraw only gains, leave principal.

Every year after retirement: Check your withdrawal rate. If you're withdrawing more than 6% of corpus annually, reduce expenses or adjust. Review healthcare bucket — if you've drawn from it, replenish from other funds.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines:
Once a year (say, every January), check if any category has drifted more than 5% from target. For example, if stocks have done well and your Large Cap allocation has grown from 15% to 20%, sell the extra 5% and move it to Debt or Overnight to restore balance.

As you enter retirement (age 60), start a gradual shift: reduce Large Cap from 15% to 5%, increase Overnight and Debt by 10%. By age 65, target 20% Aggressive Hybrid, 20% Equity Savings, 5% Large Cap, 20% Debt, 10% Gold, 25% Overnight — still growth-oriented but more protected.

Never rebalance more than once a year — frequent adjustments create taxable events and rarely improve returns.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: The equity portion of the portfolio is entirely allocated to actively managed funds (Nippon India Large Cap Fund) and hybrid funds with active equity management (ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt, HSBC Equity Savings). No index funds or ETFs are used in the equity sleeve, so the score is 0.0.

turnover_score: The plan recommends annual rebalancing only when allocations drift by more than 5%, and explicitly advises against frequent adjustments to avoid taxable events. Tactical shifts are gradual and long-term (e.g., de-risking over 5 years post-retirement). This low-turnover approach earns a high score of 0.8.

cost_emphasis_score: Expense ratios are mentioned only in passing (e.g., 1.0% for some funds), but there is no emphasis on minimising costs as a primary criterion. Fund selection appears driven by strategy, tax treatment, and risk profile rather than cost efficiency. Hence, the score is low at 0.2.

research_vs_cost_score: The rationale focuses on fund categorisation, tax treatment, and broad risk characteristics rather than deep analysis of individual stocks or fund managers. However, it does rely on active fund choices implying manager skill (e.g., Nippon Large Cap), suggesting some research-based conviction. Still, no detailed analysis is presented, so the score is 0.0 due to reliance on active funds without explicit passive philosophy.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for long-term goals (inheritance over 15+ years), with a structured glide path to retirement and beyond. Reviews are annual, and changes are gradual. This strongly aligns with a long-term horizon, scoring 0.9.

portfolio_activeness_score: All equity and hybrid funds are actively managed with no index funds. The Nippon Large Cap Fund is explicitly focused on large-cap stocks but not tracking an index. Hybrid and debt funds are actively managed. Despite missing risk metrics (beta, tracking error), the category and rationale indicate active management. Thus, the portfolio reflects active conviction, scoring 0.0.

pqs.reasoning:
The investment plan demonstrates strong alignment with Minerva McGonagall's pre-retirement, conservative profile and multi-goal framework. The 10-year horizon and upcoming retirement are well-addressed through a gradually de-risking allocation schedule (from 50% equity to 15% by age 70), with explicit rebalancing and review checkpoints. Capital preservation is prioritized via significant allocations to tax-efficient hybrid funds (Aggressive Hybrid and Equity Savings at 55% combined), which provide moderate growth with lower volatility—ideal for a conservative investor. The inclusion of Large Cap equity (15%) supports the long-term inheritance goal, while Overnight and Medium Term Debt serve liquidity and stability needs.

Diversification is strong across asset classes (equity, debt, gold), sub-categories (large cap, aggressive hybrid, equity savings, medium duration, overnight), and geographies (domestic focus appropriate for investor). Fund houses are diversified, though ICICI appears in multiple funds—moderate concentration but not excessive.

Risk-return is appropriate: the projected 8.9% base return is realistic for the allocation, and downside risks are acknowledged through STP deployment, contingency earmarking, and gradual de-risking. The plan avoids small/mid caps as per investor preference.

Internal consistency is excellent: the rationale, allocations, SIP setup, rebalancing, and review schedule form a coherent, goal-based strategy. The healthcare bucket is clearly defined across funds, and the shift to SWP post-retirement aligns with income needs.

Tax efficiency is highly optimized for the 30% tax slab: equity-taxed wrappers (Aggressive Hybrid, Equity Savings) are used for ~55% of the portfolio, avoiding slab taxation on long-term gains. Pure debt and gold (slab-taxed) are kept at moderate levels (20% combined), with clear rationale on tax treatment. ELSS is not included—but not required, as the investor’s conservative stance and lack of 80C headroom (implied) make it less suitable. Gold ETF is included with demat requirement noted. The plan correctly distinguishes equity taxation for hybrids and avoids inefficient debt routing for high-slab investor.
